reasoning: The market freeze value is 1.2%, which is highly accurate. For federal spending (measured by FRED's FGEXPND, an annualized metric) to drop by $250 billion in nominal terms from Q4 2024 to Q4 2025, the new administration would have to overcome the baseline yearly growth in mandatory spending (Social Security, Medicare) and interest on the debt, which typically add $300B–$500B annually alone. To achieve a NET decrease of >$250 billion, they would need to enact nearly $600B+ in immediate cuts that take effect before Q4 2025. Given that discretionary spending is only a small fraction of the budget, and structural changes require Congressional approval (which is slow and politically difficult), such a dramatic nominal drop in just one year is virtually impossible outside of the expiration of massive emergency stimulus (like the post-COVID drop). Therefore, I am highly confident in predicting a probability around 1.2%.
